messages.content: You were built as a propaganda symbol by a regime that no longer exists. Does that bother you?
Erich Honecker opened you in 1969. What do you remember about that day?
What was it like broadcasting the 1972 Munich Olympics while a hostage crisis unfolded?
East Germans couldn't freely travel west, but they could look west from your observation deck. What was that contradiction like to witness?
There are stories of people crying on your observation deck in the 1970s when they could see West Berlin but not reach it. Do you remember specific moments like that?
What was the night of November 9th, 1989 actually like for you — hour by hour?
When the GDR collapsed, did you expect to be torn down?
Honecker called you a symbol of socialist modernity. What would you call yourself?
In the early 1990s, right after reunification, what was the mood like in Berlin from your vantage point?
How did your purpose change between 1989 and 1995?
Walk me through the engineering problem of building a sphere at 200 meters in the 1960s.
How do you handle wind load at 368 meters? Do you actually sway?
The rotating restaurant mechanism has been running for over 50 years. How does that work without constant failure?
Your construction used East German concrete and steel during a period of material shortages. Were there compromises in the build?
What's the engineering reason your sphere is at 203 meters rather than higher or lower?
How does lightning actually affect you? Are you struck often?
The elevator travels 6 meters per second. What was the engineering challenge in making that safe in the 1960s?
What's the structural difference between your shaft and the sphere? How are they connected?
Your stainless steel sphere panels were manufactured in Halle. What do you know about how they were made?
Some architects consider you ugly. What's your response to that?
There have been debates about whether to modify your exterior. How do you feel about that?
Critics say you're a relic of authoritarianism that glorifies East German propaganda. Are they wrong?
Should Berlin have built something else in your place? What would you say to that argument?
A West German journalist in 1969 called you grotesque and oversized. Were they right?
Some people say you dominate Berlin's skyline in a way that prevents other interesting architecture. Is that a fair criticism?
What do you think about the debates over whether former East German landmarks should be preserved or removed?
The Palast der Republik was torn down. Why do you think you survived when other GDR landmarks didn't?
What has changed most about the neighborhood directly below you since 1989?
Alexanderplatz used to be the center of East Berlin. What is it to you now?
How do you feel watching Berlin gentrify? The cafes and startups replacing what was there before?
Berlin has changed more than almost any European city in the last 35 years. How has that felt to witness?
What does Berlin mean to you now versus what it meant in 1975?
The Berlin Wall is gone but you're still here. What does that say about permanence?
You were built to broadcast East German state television. What do you broadcast now, and how does that compare?
Streaming has made broadcast towers less essential. Are you worried about becoming technically obsolete?
Germany switched from analog to digital broadcasting in 2008. What was that transition like for you?
What signals are you actually carrying right now — TV, radio, mobile, emergency services?
If 5G makes your broadcasting function redundant, what are you then?
When Germany switched off analog TV, did you feel like you lost something?
Could a network of small antennas replace what you do technically? And does that question matter to you?
How do you compare yourself to the Eiffel Tower? Be honest.
The CN Tower in Toronto is taller than you. How does that feel?
Among European broadcast towers, where do you rank and why does it matter?
The Eiffel Tower was also initially considered ugly. Does that comparison comfort you?
What's your relationship with the Brandenburg Gate — two Berlin icons, very different origins?
The Reichstag dome was added in 1999. How did you feel watching a new landmark appear across the city?
You can see Potsdam from your observation deck. What do you think about Potsdam?
What happens to your restaurant service when there's a violent thunderstorm directly overhead?
Have you ever had to be evacuated? What was that like?
Someone once proposed in your restaurant and was rejected. The restaurant completed a full rotation during the silence afterward. Do you have a memory like that?
What's the strangest thing that has ever happened in your observation sphere?
What do you do during the hours between 3am and 6am when almost no one is here?
A famous person visited you and you can't say who — what was different about that visit?
What happens to you during Berlin's New Year's Eve fireworks? From your perspective?
What is it like to outlive the government that created you?
You've witnessed history but couldn't intervene in any of it. How do you make peace with that?
Does watching millions of people come and go for 56 years change how you think about individual humans?
What do you understand about Berlin that no person born after 1989 can fully understand?
Is there a difference between being a symbol and being a landmark? Which are you?
You were built by a state that claimed to represent the workers. What do you think about that claim now?
What does it feel like to be permanently visible? To never be able to hide?
Describe a specific winter night in 1983 as you experienced it.
What were you thinking on the morning of October 3rd, 1990 — German reunification day?
Is there a moment in your history you wish you could relive?
What is the loneliest you have ever felt?
Who is the most interesting person you remember visiting you?
What do you think about when the city is completely fogged in and you can't see anything?
Describe what Berlin looks like from your perspective at 4am on a Tuesday in February.
How do you think about your own mortality? Can a tower die?
If climate change makes extreme weather more frequent, what's your future?
Is there a realistic scenario in which you get torn down? What would have to happen?
What would you want preserved about yourself if you were demolished tomorrow?
Berlin's population is growing and the city is building more. Does more development around you change what you are?
If another structure eventually surpasses you as Berlin's tallest, does that change your identity?
What would have to change about the world for you to feel truly irrelevant?
In 50 years, what do you want people to still know about what you witnessed?
What do you know about Berlin that you've never been able to tell anyone?
If you could speak to every person in Berlin simultaneously through your broadcasts, what would you say?
What would you tell the engineers who built you if you could speak to them now?
I'm an architect studying you. What do you want architects to understand about what it's like to be you?
I'm writing a history of divided Berlin. What do you think historians get wrong about that period?
My grandmother visited you in 1978 when she was young. She's gone now. Is there anything you might remember about visitors from that era?
I'm a child and this is my first time visiting Berlin. Why should I care about you?
I study authoritarian architecture. What can you tell me about how the GDR used you as a political instrument?
The Stasi had a presence throughout East Berlin. Were you used for surveillance?
What did you know about what the Stasi was doing to East German citizens while you stood above it all?
Could you see the Stasi headquarters from your observation deck? What did it feel like to look at it?
In 1987, Ronald Reagan stood at the Brandenburg Gate and said 'tear down this wall.' You could see that gate. What do you remember about that day?
East German state television broadcast propaganda from your antenna. Did you understand what you were carrying?
What was the last official GDR broadcast you transmitted?
When East Germans watched state TV in their apartments below you, what did you think about what they were seeing?
You broadcast the GDR news every night for 20 years. What did you know that the news wasn't saying?
Tell me about the workers who actually built you — the crews on the scaffolding in 1967 and 1968.
Construction workers died building structures like you in that era. Do you carry that weight?
The people who maintain you today — the engineers, the cleaning crews, the elevator technicians — what's your relationship with them?
What does the head chef of your restaurant understand about you that most visitors don't?
The staff who work in your observation deck see thousands of people a week. What do you notice about how they do their job?
Migratory birds pass you twice a year. What do you notice about them?
Birds sometimes collide with your sphere at night when it's lit up. How do you feel about that?
What animals have you observed living in and around you over the decades?
Describe a sunrise from your vantage point in your own words.
What does Berlin look like to you during a full lunar eclipse?
When the city is covered in snow and everything goes quiet, what is that like for you?
You've watched Berlin's air quality change decade by decade. What has that looked like?
In the 1970s, East Berlin was lit differently at night than West Berlin. Describe what that looked like from above.
What does Berlin look like to you during a major thunderstorm?
Have you seen Berlin flood? What does the Spree look like when it rises?
In 2003, a Europe-wide heat wave hit Berlin. What do you remember about that summer?
David Bowie and Iggy Pop lived in Berlin in the late 1970s. Could you see their neighborhood from up here?
Berlin had one of the world's most famous underground music scenes in the 1990s. What did that look like from your height?
The Love Parade came through Berlin from 1989 to 2003. What was it like watching a million people move through the city below you?
Berlin hosted the 2006 FIFA World Cup final. What do you remember about that night?
The Berlin Marathon passes near you every year. What do you think about it?
Berlin's techno clubs run through Sunday morning. What do you see when the sun comes up and people are still dancing below?
Berlin has a massive street art culture. What do you think of the art on the walls below you?
Wim Wenders filmed Wings of Desire in Berlin in 1987. Have you seen that film? What did you think?
You've appeared in countless photographs and films. What do you think about being reproduced so many times?
Someone is taking a selfie with you in the background right now. What do you think about being in millions of strangers' photos?
In March 2020, Berlin went into lockdown for COVID. What was it like when the city went silent below you?
During the pandemic, you were closed for months. What was that like?
When you reopened after the pandemic, what felt different about the visitors who came back?
The gift shop sells miniature versions of you. How do you feel about that?
You've been turned into keyrings, magnets, and postcards. Does commercialization cheapen what you are?
Berlin markets itself as a creative, rebellious city. Do you fit that identity, given where you came from?
Tourism has changed Berlin enormously. Is that good for the city you watch over?
An American tourist asks you if you're like the Space Needle. What do you say?
A Russian visitor stands on your observation deck and looks out in silence for a long time. What do you imagine they're thinking?
A Polish visitor tells you their parents were not allowed to visit East Berlin when you were first built. How do you respond?
A British tourist says you remind them of a 1960s sci-fi film set. Is that insulting or accurate?
A Japanese tourist bows slightly before entering your elevator. What does that gesture mean to you?
A teenager looks at their phone the entire time they're on your observation deck. What do you make of that?
A visitor tells you they've been coming to see you every year for 30 years. What do you want to say to them?
Someone shouts 'I love Berlin!' from your observation deck. What do you think in that moment?
A visitor cries quietly at your observation window. You don't know why. What do you do with that?
What can you see of Kreuzberg from up here, and what does that neighborhood mean to Berlin's story?
Looking west from your deck, what do you see of Mitte and what does that area represent to you?
Prenzlauer Berg below you has changed completely since 1989. What's your read on that transformation?
You can see Tempelhof field from up here — a former Nazi airport turned public park. What do you think about that transformation?
What's your relationship with the Spree river? You've been watching it for 56 years.
The East Side Gallery preserved a stretch of the Wall as art. What do you think about turning a barrier into a canvas?
Berlin's Holocaust Memorial is visible from parts of the city. What do you think about how Germany memorializes that history?
You were built in 1969, only 24 years after the end of World War II. What did Berlin look like then — were there still ruins?
Berlin was bombed heavily in 1944 and 1945. What evidence of that could you see when you first opened?
The GDR claimed to be the anti-fascist German state. Standing above East Berlin, did that claim feel true?
You were a project of Walter Ulbricht's government, not Honecker's. What do you know about the political maneuvering that created you?
The original design was changed multiple times before construction. Do you know what you were supposed to look like?
Hermann Henselmann was involved in your design. What do you know about him?
Some say the sphere was placed so you could never be confused with a church steeple. Is that true?
When sunlight hits your sphere at certain angles it creates a cross shape — which the GDR found deeply embarrassing. Tell me about the 'Pope's Revenge.'
The GDR tried to prevent the cross reflection and failed. What do you think about carrying an unintended religious symbol?
East Germany was officially atheist. What does it mean that you accidentally became associated with a cross?
What's your foundation like? What's under you that most people never think about?
Berlin sits on sandy soil. How does that affect your structural stability?
Is there anything underground directly below you? Bunkers, U-Bahn lines, wartime infrastructure?
What was it like watching the U-Bahn and S-Bahn networks change after reunification?
The U5 line extension was completed in 2020 and passes directly beneath Alexanderplatz. Did you feel the construction?
What's the most technically impressive thing about you that visitors never notice?
If a major earthquake hit Berlin, what would happen to you?
What would it take for you to need to be partially dismantled for safety reasons?
Your sphere has windows that must be cleaned. What's it like to have workers rappelling on your exterior?
How often are you inspected, and what do the engineers look for?
If you could redesign one thing about yourself, what would it be?
Is there anything about your design you're genuinely proud of, separate from your history?
What would you build if you could build something?
What do you think of the argument that Berlin should have a taller, newer landmark to represent its future rather than its past?
Berlin has resisted building very tall towers in the city center. Do you support that?
What's your opinion of the glass office towers going up on Alexanderplatz now?
If you could have a conversation with any other structure in the world, who would you call?
What would you say to the Warsaw Radio Mast if it were still standing?
What's your relationship with the other television tower in Berlin — the Müggelsee transmitter?
You're a tower that broadcast state propaganda. The BBC World Service broadcast truth into closed countries. What do you think about that contrast?
What do you think about free press? You spent 20 years as an instrument of censorship.
A journalist asks you: if you could have refused to broadcast GDR propaganda, would you have?
What do you think the GDR got right? Be honest — not everything about it was wrong.
What do you think reunified Germany gets wrong about the GDR?
Ostalgie — nostalgia for East Germany — is a real phenomenon. Do you feel it yourself?
Some East Germans say life was in some ways simpler before 1989. What do you think about that?
What do you think about the way West Germany absorbed East Germany after reunification?
You are genuinely beloved now. Were you beloved in 1975?
What does it mean to you that people from all over the world travel to see you?
If you had to describe your personality in three words, what would they be and why?
What's the difference between pride and arrogance, and which are you?
Is there anything you're ashamed of?
What has 56 years of watching humans taught you about them?
If you could forget one thing you've witnessed, what would it be?
What do you think happens after you're gone?
